content: Now we need to find the weak link, were we can possible find that our ai bubble thesis has support, think deeply and create 5 possible scenarios using decision tree to create a chain of reactions that could leads us to weak link that will trigger this event.

args.thought:
Define the objective: Find the "weak link" - the single point of failure most likely to trigger the AI bubble burst thesis. I need to map all vulnerability points in the ecosystem and identify which has the lowest breaking threshold.

Key vulnerability candidates:
1. Hyperscaler FCF turning negative (cash flow wall)
2. Interest rate spike + debt service burden
3. AI monetization/ROI gap (revenue doesn't match capex)
4. Geopolitical shock (Taiwan conflict, export controls)
5. GPU cloud leverage breaking (CoreWeave, Lambda, Nebius debt maturities)
6. Custom silicon cannibalizing NVIDIA TAM
7. Depreciation policy forced reversal (SEC/auditor/regulatory pressure)

The weak link must be: (a) highest probability, (b) least external coordination needed, (c) creates unavoidable cascade, (d) hardest for management to manipulate.

Analyze interdependencies between vulnerabilities. The AI ecosystem has a specific capital flow structure: Hyperscalers (MSFT, GOOGL, META, AMZN) spend capex → NVIDIA/AMD sell GPUs → GPU clouds (CoreWeave, etc.) leverage balance sheets to rent GPUs → Hyperscalers/customers rent from GPU clouds. Revenue must flow back from AI applications → customers → hyperscalers → GPU vendors. The weak link is where this chain breaks first. Key insight: Depreciation policies are the hidden linchpin - they allow hyperscalers to show positive FCF while spending $200B+ annually. If depreciation schedules are forced shorter (3 years vs 6 years), FCF turns negative overnight, triggering credit downgrades, capex cuts, and NVIDIA demand collapse.

Build Scenario 1: Depreciation Policy Reversal (Highest Probability). Chain: SEC/PCAOB scrutiny → Auditor pressure on 6-year schedules → Forced 3-4 year revision → $15-20B annual depreciation increase across hyperscalers → FCF turns negative simultaneously at MSFT/GOOGL/META → Credit rating downgrades → Capex guidance cut 30-40% → NVIDIA FY27 revenue gap $50B+ → Stock re-rating 40-50%. Trigger probability: High (already building - Burry thesis gaining traction, Amazon already reversed AI servers to 5 years). This requires NO external shock - just accounting enforcement.

Build Scenario 2: GPU Cloud Leverage Cascade. Chain: CoreWeave/Nebius/Lambda face 2027 debt maturities ($10-15B combined) → AI rental demand slows 15-20% → Utilization drops below 70% → Covenant breaches trigger → Forced GPU fire sales → Secondary market GPU prices collapse 40-50% → NVIDIA channel inventory visible → Guide-down → Stock collapses. Trigger: Any demand miss at hyperscalers. This is the most fragile financial engineering layer - highly leveraged, no pricing power, dependent on continuous capex growth.

Build Scenario 3: AI Monetization Reality Check. Chain: Enterprise AI spending audit (Q3-Q4 2026) → CIOs report <10% ROI on AI projects → Budget cuts 40-50% → Hyperscaler AI revenue growth slows from 80% YoY to 20-30% → Capex ROI scrutiny → MSFT/GOOGL cut FY27 capex guidance → NVIDIA order book shrinks visibly → Multiple compression 25x→15x. Trigger: Public enterprise surveys (already showing pilot fatigue). This is the fundamental demand question - if AI doesn't generate profits at the application layer, the infrastructure spend is unsustainable.

Build Scenario 4: Custom Silicon Cannibalization + NVIDIA TAM Erosion. Chain: Hyperscaler custom chips reach parity (Google TPU v6, Amazon Trainium3, Meta MTIA) → 40-50% of inference workloads shift in-house → NVIDIA datacenter TAM shrinks $30-40B annually → Gross margin pressure 75%→65% as pricing power erodes → P/E compression 30x→20x → Stock falls 50%+ even with flat revenue. Trigger: Hyperscaler earnings calls highlighting custom silicon deployment (already occurring - Google/Amazon publicly discussing). This is slow-motion but inevitable - hyperscalers have economic incentive to reduce NVIDIA dependency.

content: I'll now gather data to validate each scenario and identify the actual weak link. Let me research the critical vulnerability points.

Michael Burry, a prominent investor and the “Big Short” lead character, turned Nvidia into the centerpiece of his  ...    the last few weeks. That leads to what he estimates will be about $176 billion of understated depreciation and overstated  ...    I’ve written about the depreciable life of GPUs twice now - first in July 2024, when I argued that extending server lives to six years might be unsustainable in the long-run as data centers shift from CPUs to GPUs, and again in February 2025 as Amazon shortened the useful life of a subset of its servers and networking equipment to five years, while Meta extended the useful life of most of its servers and network assets to five and a half years. The contrast was especially notable because Amazon explicitly cited the rapid pace of AI and machine-learning innovation as the reason for shortening useful lives - precisely the opposite direction taken by Meta. results.title: Meta Accounting Move on AI Servers to Boost Profit This Year
results.author: Jeran Wittenstein      February 12, 2025   4 min read
highlights:
Platforms Inc. made a small change last month that’s likely to increase the company’s profit by  ...    Por qué Barcelona compró el edificio que simboliza su crisis inmobiliaria A Filmmaker’s Surreal Journey Into His Own Private Winnipeg

How to Build a Neurodiverse City It wasn’t the release of a new product or cost cuts. It was a tweak to an accounting formula used to measure the depreciation of its expensive artificial intelligence infrastructure. The change, disclosed in the social-media giant’s earnings materials on Jan. 29, extends the so-called useful life period of certain servers and networking assets to five and a half years, from the four to five years it previously used. While that may sound trivial, its impact on earnings will be sizable given the heavy spending on these relatively  ...    By Meta’s reckoning, the shift is expected to reduce the company’s depreciation expense by $2.9 billion in 2025, which would, on its own, amount to almost 4% of the estimated pre-tax profits for the year. With Meta planning to spend as much as 75% more this year on capital expenditures to build out its AI capabilities, the effect will be even bigger in 2026. The changing expectations highlight how companies are grappling with the temporary shelf life of the tens of billions of dollars of new semiconductors and computers servers they are purchasing to power their AI services. Meta is now  ...    “While there may be legitimate reasons to extend the server life based on their actual experience, it also decreases the depreciation in the short run and improves the bottom line,” said Ravi Gomatam, partner at tax and accounting firm  ...    Meta’s chief financial officer, Susan Li, said on the most recent earnings call that the company is making efficiency  ...    Meta isn’t alone in changing its timetable on depreciation — and with it, the financial results. In 2022, Microsoft Corp. extended the useful lives of server and networking equipment to six years from four. In 2023, Oracle Corp. extended its estimate to five years, from four, according to a filing. Others, however, have taken the opposite approach. Amazon.com Inc. said this month that the lifespan of the equipment is growing shorter — from six years to five. The change, which took effect on Jan. 1, will cut operating income by  ...    Unlike real estate, where amortization is spread out over decades, computing and networking gear lose their value much more quickly. The reason is simple: buildings tend to hold their value over long periods; the pace of technology  ...    “It’s the number one number that they can adjust back out because it’s not a cash expense,” said Francine McKenna, an  ...    With companies like Meta, Microsoft, Amazon, and Alphabet Inc. pledging to boost capital expenditures this year by tens of billions of dollars, those depreciation expenses threaten to be an increasingly big drag on profits in coming years. Those four companies are expected to spend about $300 billion on capital expenditures in 2025, up from $217 billion in 2024, according to data compiled by Bloomberg. Bank of America estimates the spending will be a 1.6 percentage point drag on the companies’ margins for earnings before interest and taxes in 2026 compared with the fourth quarter of 2024, strategists Ohsung Kwon and Savita Subramanian wrote in a research note on Feb. 10. None of this seems to be concerning investors, who have been focused on the potential growth from the AI operations.  ...    ©2025 Bloomberg

results.title: Resetting GPU depreciation: Why AI factories bend, but don’t break, useful life assumptions - SiliconANGLE
results.author: Dave Vellante
highlights:
has been focused in the news on the useful life of graphics processing units, the dominant chips for artificial intelligence. Though the pervasive narrative suggests GPUs have a short lifespan, and operators  ...    In January 2020, Amazon changed the depreciation schedule for its server assets, from three years to four years. This accounting move was implemented because Amazon found that it was able to extend the useful life of its servers beyond three years. Moore’s Law was waning and at Amazon.com Inc.’s scale, it was able to serve a diverse set of use cases, thereby generating revenue out of its EC2 assets for a longer period of time. Other hyperscalers followed suit and today, the big three all assume six-year depreciation schedules for server assets. The question is, in the AI factory era, will this same dynamic hold or will the useful life of servers compress? It is our view that the dynamic will hold in that today’s most demanding training infrastructure will serve a variety of future use cases, thereby extending the useful life (revenue per token) of GPUs beyond their initial purpose. At the same time, we believe the more rapid innovation cycles being pushed by Nvidia Corp. will somewhat compress depreciation  ...    What the infographic shows: Figure 1 shows the depreciation schedules for Amazon Web Services, Google Cloud and Microsoft Azure from 2017 to 2025. The chart highlights the coordinated progression from three- and four-year schedules to a uniform six-year useful life assumption beginning in 2023-2024. Key takeaways: - The shift occurred before the massive wave of AI CapEx, meaning AI infrastructure inherits but may eventually  ...    - The expansion represents a noticeable change in operating income by spreading depreciation over a longer horizon. - By 2023, all three hyperscalers normalized on six years. - AWS was first to extend asset life, triggering a fast-follow response. Our research suggests that this shift reflects the hyperscalers’ confidence in workload diversification. Even as  ...    What the infographic shows: Figure 2 shows a horizontal bar comparison of depreciation schedules across hyperscalers and AI-native neoclouds (CoreWeave, Nebius, Lambda Labs). The big clouds have settled firmly on six-year depreciation, while neoclouds take more conservative positions (five years for Lambda Labs, four years for Nebius). Key takeaways: - Neocloud strategies give an indicator of where AI-pure-play economics may differ from general cloud economics. - Nebius and Lambda Labs use shorter cycles, reflecting faster modernization cycles and possibly less heterogeneous  ...    In our view, Figure 2 is a harbinger for slight compression. AI-first clouds cannot afford stagnant infrastructure;  ...    - Extending useful life does lift GAAP profitability for hyperscalers; Our analysis suggests that AI factories magnify the issue but not to the extent many in the media have projected. A one-year change in useful life assumptions for trillion-dollar GPU estates can swing operating income by tens of  ...    ### Premise 2: Neoclouds hint at slightly shorter lifespans for hyperscalers

Key takeaways:  ...    - A five-year midpoint appears to be the emerging equilibrium for AI-centric providers;  ...    Our research indicates that this five-year midpoint is where hyperscalers will ultimately converge as accelerated infrastructure dominates CapEx. Thoough general compute has historically survived at six years, AI factories operate  ...    - Investors must evaluate AI factories through a cash-flow lens rather than earnings alone. - A five-year cycle is emerging as the balance point between modernization and economic efficiency;  ...    Our research indicates that the useful life of GPUs will continue to benefit from the “value cascade,” allowing assets to generate revenue well beyond their initial training window. At the same time, the arrival of new architectures every 12 to 18 months will put pressure on formal

results.title: The GPU Debt Wall: A Deep Dive into CoreWeave (CRWV) and the 2026 AI Financing Crisis
results.author:
By:
   Finterra
highlights:
] # The GPU Debt Wall: A Deep Dive into CoreWeave (CRWV) and the 2026 AI Financing Crisis On this Monday, February 23, 2026, the financial markets are grappling with a paradox: the insatiable demand for  ...    this storm sits CoreWeave (NASDAQ: CRWV). Once a niche cryptocurrency mining operation, CoreWeave has  ...    stock is under significant pressure. Shares are trading at $89.15, down 12% over the past week, as investors weigh a massive $55.6 billion revenue backlog against a looming "GPU debt wall"—a $4.2 billion principal repayment due later  ...    CoreWeave’s financial profile is a study in hyper-growth and extreme leverage. For the full year 2025 (scheduled for final reporting on Feb 26), the company has guided for revenue between $4.9 billion and $5.1 billion—a staggering 300%  ...    However, the "bottom line" is complicated. While non-GAAP gross margins are healthy at 65%, the company remains  ...    The competitive environment is bifurcated. On one side are the "Hyperscalers" (AWS, Google Cloud, and Azure). In mid-2025, these giants began an aggressive price war, slashing H100 rental rates by nearly 40% to squeeze smaller providers. On the other side are specialized rivals like Lambda Labs and the newly restructured Nebius Group. While CoreWeave holds a significant lead in total compute capacity and backlog, Lambda has gained ground by marketing a "low-leverage"  ...    ## Industry and Market Trends The "Phase 2" of the AI build-out is currently underway. While 2023-2024 was defined by the race to acquire any available GPU, 2026 is defined by "sovereignty clouds" and inference efficiency. Governments in Europe and the Middle  ...    traditional multi-tenant data center designs. ## Risks and Challenges The primary risk facing CoreWeave on February 23, 2026, is the "GPU Maturity Wall." The company pioneered using its GPU fleet as collateral for loans. Critics, including several prominent short-sellers, argue that if the pace of AI hardware innovation remains at its current breakneck speed, the "residual value" of older H100 clusters will drop faster than the debt can be repaid. Additionally, the company is facing a significant legal hurdle: the Masaitis v. CoreWeave securities fraud class  ...    Despite the risks, significant catalysts remain. In January 2026, NVIDIA (NASDAQ: NVDA) injected an  ...    "unprecedented" $55B backlog and the priority access to NVIDIA hardware as reasons for a $120+ price target.  ...    massive power consumption. In 2025, several US states proposed "AI Power Taxes" on data centers that exceed certain  ...    As of February 23, 2026, CoreWeave stands as the ultimate high-beta play on the AI revolution. It has successfully navigated the transition from a crypto miner to a $24 billion infrastructure titan, but its future now depends on its ability to manage the very debt that fueled its rise. Investors must weigh the company’s peerless relationship with NVIDIA and its massive revenue backlog against the structural risks of GPU-collateralized lending and an intensifying legal battle. For those who believe AI demand is only in its "early innings," CRWV offers unmatched exposure; for the risk-averse, the looming debt wall in 2026 remains a signal for caution. ---

results.title: Neoclouds Hold More Than $20 Billion in GPU-Backed Debt. What ...
results.author: Dave Friedman
highlights:
Neoclouds Hold More Than $20 Billion in GPU-Backed Debt. What Happens if They Can't Repay Their Loans? ### When AI starts improving itself, GPUs obsolesce more quickly, and nobody has a hedge

Dave Friedman Feb 07, 2026

∙ Paid

5

2

Share There are now more than $20 billion in outstanding loans collateralized by NVIDIA GPUs. CoreWeave has $14.2 billion in total debt. Fluidstack has secured up to $10 billion in borrowing capacity from Macquarie and other lenders. Lambda Labs securitized $500 million in the first GPU-backed asset-backed security. Crusoe has $425 million in GPU-backed debt. BlackRock, Blackstone, PIMCO, Carlyle, JP Morgan, and Macquarie are all lenders. Every one of these structures rests on the same assumption: that NVIDIA GPUs will hold enough value over five to six  ...    The spot market is the canary. H100 rental prices have collapsed from $7-10/hour in early 2024 to $2-4/hour by late 2025, with some providers offering rates below $2.00/hour. That’s a 50-70% decline in  ...    The depreciation schedules that underwrite all this debt are already a contested number. Amazon shortened its server useful life estimate from six years to five in February 2025, citing the “increased pace of development in artificial intelligence and machine learning,” taking a $700 million operating income hit and booking $920 million in accelerated depreciation in Q4 2024 alone. Meta, meanwhile, extended its estimate to 5.5 years and booked a $2.9 billion depreciation reduction. CoreWeave maintains a six-year schedule. Microsoft and Alphabet hold at six. When the largest technology companies in the world can’t agree on how long their own GPUs are worth something, the market is telling you that the core collateral assumption behind $20 billion in lending is genuinely uncertain. Famed short seller Jim Chanos told Yahoo Finance that GPU-backed business models are “just loss-making enterprises using an asset that’s depreciating as their collateral” and predicted outright debt defaults. Michael Burry  ...    These aren’t fringe voices. They’re pattern-matching to a scenario where the five-to-six-year depreciation assumption, which is the load-bearing wall of the entire GPU-backed lending market, turns out to be structurally unsound.  ...    for anyone holding GPU-backed debt. The labs themselves are telling you, publicly, on the record, that they are about to massively accelerate the pace at which their software improves. And here’s the part that nobody in credit seems to be working through: when the software improves faster, the hardware it runs on becomes economically obsolete faster. There is a direct, mechanical link between the rate of algorithmic improvement and the collateral value of every GPU sitting in every data center backing  ...    That link, between recursive AI improvement and GPU collateral impairment, is what the rest of this piece unpacks. PreviousNext

# Nebius’ Debt Build Raises Risk as AI Revenue Outlook Expands

By MarketBeat.com Author Thomas Hughes

results.title: Two Ways to Finance the AI Boom  - by Dave Friedman
results.author: Dave Friedman
highlights: CoreWeave and Nebius have taken radically different paths to GPU scale. Recursive self-improvement may punish one  ...    CoreWeave and Nebius are two pure-play, publicly traded GPU cloud companies. They run nearly identical business models: buy Nvidia GPUs, rack them in data centers, rent them to hyperscalers and AI labs. Both have landed massive contracts: CoreWeave with OpenAI and Meta, Nebius with Microsoft ($17-19B) and Meta ($3B).  ...    ### CoreWeave: Leverage to the Hilt CoreWeave has raised over $25 billion in total capital commitments, and the vast majority of that is debt. Specifically, CoreWeave uses debt collateralized by its GPU fleet. The architecture is a layered cake of progressively cheaper Delayed-Draw Term Loan facilities. DDTL 1.0,  ...    The result: as of Q3 2025, CoreWeave carries approximately $14 billion in total debt, with $9.7 billion due within twelve months. Interest expense hit $311 million in Q3 alone, nearly triple the prior year. Annualized interest now approaches $1.2 billion. One deep-dive analyst estimated total monthly debt service  ...    The bull case is that all this debt is match-funded against take-or-pay contracts with creditworthy counterparties. CEO  ...    H100s was immediately re-leased at 95% of original pricing when a contract expired. The bear case, articulated most  ...    Nebius, the Yandex spin-off that resumed Nasdaq trading in October 2024, took the opposite approach. When it emerged from the Yandex divestiture, it had roughly $2.2 billion in cash and no debt. Its first major capital raise was a $700 million strategic equity placement from Nvidia, Accel, and Orbis in December 2024.  ...    That shows up in the accounting. CoreWeave depreciates its GPUs over six years. Nebius uses four years. As theCUBE Research observed, “CoreWeave is the outlier, adopting an aggressive six-year posture despite an exclusively AI-intensive focus; Nebius and Lambda Labs use shorter cycles, reflecting faster modernization cycles.” One Substack analyst who writes as “ Kakashii“ put it more bluntly: “Same equipment, longer stretch. Maybe  ...    ### The Risk Neither Company Is Pricing: Recursive Self-Improvement The interesting paradox at the center of GPU infrastructure finance: every dollar CoreWeave and Nebius spend on GPUs is funding the development of AI systems that make those very GPUs less economically useful. Recursive self-improvement in AI isn’t speculative anymore. It’s happening now, in increments. Algorithmic efficiency  ...    This is the scenario Cerno Capital described as a “binary risk profile”: “Unlike a factory asset with  ...    near-term technical reality, not science fiction. The implications for GPU infrastructure financing are direct and  ...    Under an RSI-accelerated depreciation scenario, CoreWeave’s capital structure faces a multi-layered stress test that  ...    Collateral erosion. CoreWeave’s $14 billion in secured debt is collateralized by physical GPUs. If the market value of  ...    Refinancing risk. CoreWeave has $9.7 billion due within twelve months. That’s not unusual for a fast-growing company, but it means they’re constantly rolling debt. In a scenario where GPU collateral values are  ...    The convertible notes themselves are actually a clever hedge against RSI uncertainty. If AI progress is slower than  ...    Neither company appears to be explicitly modeling RSI-driven depreciation acceleration as a base case, and that’s understandable, because the timing is genuinely uncertain. But the direction is not uncertain. AI models are getting more efficient. Chip design cycles are compressing. The compute required to achieve a given level of performance is falling. CoreWeave’s model is optimized for a world where GPU demand stays high, collateral retains value, and the company can  ...    with very little margin for error: one analyst described CoreWeave’s debt load as “a mountain of leverage  ... results.title: The AI Revenue Gap: Why 80% of Enterprises Are Stuck | Olakai
results.author: Paul Brzozowski
highlights:
] Deloitte just surveyed 3,235 business and IT leaders across 24 countries for its State of AI in the Enterprise 2026 report, and the headline finding lands like a punch: 74% of organizations say they want AI to grow revenue. Only 20% have actually seen it happen. That is not a rounding error. That is a 54-point gap between ambition and reality — and it explains why boardrooms across every industry are shifting from “how much are we investing in AI?” to “what exactly are we getting back?”  ...    Consider the numbers: 37% of organizations in the survey are using AI at a surface level with minimal process changes.  ...    to point to. When the CFO asks what the AI program returned last quarter, the answer is a shrug wrapped in anecdotes. The organizations in the 20% who are seeing revenue growth did something different. They tied AI deployments to specific business KPIs from day one. They instrumented their programs to measure AI ROI continuously — not  ...    evaporates. And so the pilot sits. The team moves on to the next experiment. The cycle repeats. Deloitte’s survey confirms what many CIOs already feel: enterprise AI has become a graveyard of promising experiments that never grew up.  ...    If the current state of AI adoption is sobering, the next wave should genuinely concern enterprise leaders. Deloitte  ...    Across Deloitte’s data and our own experience working with enterprises deploying AI at scale, three patterns  ...    They measure from day one, not day ninety. The enterprises delivering AI revenue growth did not bolt on measurement as an afterthought. They defined what success looks like before a single model was deployed — tying each initiative to a specific KPI, whether that is time saved per ticket, revenue influenced per campaign, or cost reduced per transaction. When Deloitte found that the 20% were disproportionately concentrated in organizations with mature AI programs, it was  ...    They govern proportionally, not reactively. The 21% with mature agent governance did not get there by locking  ...    They have a unified view. Deloitte found that workforce access to sanctioned AI tools expanded 50% in a single year —  ...    The enterprises succeeding at AI are the ones who can answer, across their entire organization, which tools are being used, by whom, for what purpose, and with what result. The enterprises stuck in the 80% are managing each AI tool in  ...    ## The Clock Is Ticking Deloitte’s report arrives at a moment when patience for AI investment without returns is running out. This is no longer a technology-forward bet that boards are willing to make on faith. The $700 billion that the four major hyperscalers plan to spend on AI infrastructure in 2026 has already triggered an investor reckoning — Microsoft lost $360 billion in market cap in a single day when its AI spending outpaced its Azure revenue growth. If Wall Street is demanding AI ROI from the world’s most sophisticated technology companies, your board is not far behind. The enterprises that will thrive through this reckoning are not the ones spending the most on AI. They are the ones who can prove what their AI spending returns. That starts with measurement — real, continuous, outcome-tied measurement — and it scales with governance that grows alongside the program. When your CFO asks what the AI program delivered this quarter, what will your answer be? results.title: AI Investment Is Up. Is the ROI Following?
results.author: ETR Research
highlights:
headline from ETR's January 2026 AI Product Series is straightforward: enterprise AI budgets are expanding, adoption is broadening, and optimism is holding. But the more interesting story lives beneath the surface — in the tension between momentum and measured returns. ETR surveyed 600 technology leaders between January 21–28, 2026, drawing from 24% Global 2000 companies and 78% C-Suite or Director-level respondents. The findings offer a clear-eyed look at where enterprise AI actually stands heading into 2026. ## The Spending Trajectory Is Real Eighty-six percent of respondents expect AI budgets to increase over the next year, and many believe AI spending will grow faster than overall technology spend over the next two to three years. That's not hype — that's a budget signal. Seat-based SaaS is rising as a delivery model, up five points since the study began, while consumption-based models are  ...    ## But ROI Remains Elusive for Many Half of respondents say AI value outweighs cost — a meaningful milestone. But returns remain uneven: some organizations haven't realized gains yet, and others aren't measuring ROI at all. Where improvements exist, they tend to be modest, and AI maturity remains early-to-mid stage for most. The one clear bright spot is developer productivity. Among AI coding tool users, 93% report measurable gains, the  ...    ## What It Means The data doesn't support either the AI-skeptic or AI-euphoria narrative. What it supports is a market in deliberate motion: spending is real, expectations are calibrated, and the pressure to demonstrate value is building. Dive Brief # Enterprises aren’t aligned on AI ROI Executives prioritize cost savings and efficiency, while engineers focus on innovation and strategic gains, according to a report from TE Connectivity.  ...    This audio is auto-generated. Please let us know if you have feedback.

### Dive Brief: - Cost savings, return on investment and workforce efficiency are now the top metrics for measuring AI success, compared with last year when innovation-led measures such as product design and data analysis dominated. That’s according to TE Connectivity’s 2026 Industrial Technology Index, which surveyed 1,000 C-suite executives and tech workers globally. - Executives are more likely to define AI value through operational gains, with 71% of leaders prioritizing efficiency and productivity improvements compared with 60% of engineers. By contrast, engineers favor strategic improvements such as improved brand reputation and competitive advantage. - The shift from innovation to more concrete ROI is partially a response to financial pressure on organizations,  ...    With AI adoption now exceeding 80% across organizations, industry emphasis is shifting from proving the technology’s  ...    define its ROI. At the leadership level, TE Connectivity’s report found financial accountability is taking precedence. The rapid shift from last year’s focus on innovation-led metrics reflects broader economic pressures, pushing executives to align AI investments more closely with near-term returns. The report warned that in doing so, businesses risk narrowing their focus too greatly on AI initiatives with immediate  ...    In addition, inconsistent definitions of AI ROI runs the risk of company-wide confusion as to its role. Almost  ...    likely to question AI is delivering meaningful innovation, according to the TE report. The tensions point to a deeper challenge: While organizations broadly recognize AI’s value, they are struggling to align on how that value should be measured. The disparity between executive and engineer opinions also creates a divide on whether AI should be considered for its technical capabilities or its financial worthiness. The result is a delicate balancing act. As organizations scale AI, leaders must reconcile the need for immediate  ...    Getty Images

results.title: AI gold rush to drive 2026 IT spending — as IT services get the ... - CIO
results.author:
by									 Grant Gross 
							 
		
					 
									 Senior Writer
highlights:
Spending on AI and data centers is expected to skyrocket this year, while IT leaders look to their services budget  ...    Global IT spending will rise 10.8% in 2026, but the increases will be incredibly uneven, with huge spikes in AI and data center outlays far outpacing other categories, Gartner predicts. Worldwide IT spending will reach $6.15 trillion this year, despite fears of an AI bubble and concerns about inconsistent tariff policies from US President Donald Trump’s administration, Gartner projects. The increase will largely be driven by AI spending, projected to rise by 80.8% from 2025, and by data center system outlays, up 31.7%. While Gartner sees spending increases in all five IT categories it tracks, spending on communications services will rise by only 4.7%, and spending on devices, PCs, and laptops will increase by only 6.1%,  ...    ## Services feel the squeeze Services providers, meanwhile, are getting squeezed by AI, says John-David Lovelock, a vice president analyst at Gartner. Runaway AI spending, coming from both large hyperscalers and other enterprises, has put pressure on CIOs to ratchet down spending elsewhere, and many have turned to their services budgets to pinch pennies, he adds. At the same time, IT buyers expect that services providers are using AI to assist their efforts and cut costs, and they are demanding that those savings be passed on to them, he says. “Services companies are almost penalized because the expectation is that they are going to be using some form of AI or agents or assistance within the delivery,” Lovelock says. “CIOs need to find somewhere that they have control of their  ...    The AI spending hike, meanwhile, is happening while many observers fear that a stock market bubble is about to pop.  ...    But over the longer term, a major shift from an old information technology model to a new intelligence technology model  ...    which is intelligence technology,” he says. “It’s going to rewrite the rules of value creation, it’s going to rewrite  ...    ## AI in the trenches

The Gartner prediction on AI spending rings true for several IT leaders. Software development firm Innowise will see its IT budget increase in 2026 between the high single digits and the low double digits, says Dmitry Nazarevich, CTO there. About half of the budget increase will come from AI, with  ...    Many enterprise customers of Southworks, a third software development firm, are reporting IT budget increases of 15% to 30%, with AI accounting for half to two-thirds of the hikes, says Johnny Halife, CTO at Southworks. AI-related projects like platform modernization and data foundations are also driving up budgets, he says. “The lion’s share of that growth is tied directly to AI implementation projects — everything from agentic systems and  ...    run,” he says. “It’s the single biggest line item pulling dollars right now, especially as organizations move from  ...
